Make (Integromat) vs n8n: Which Is Better?

Choosing between Make (Integromat) and n8n? Here's a side-by-side comparison to help you decide.

Quick verdict: Choose Make (Integromat) if you need freelancers or small-teams. Choose n8n if you prioritize developers or solo-devs.

Make (Integromat)

PricingFree / $9/mo Pro / $16/mo Teams
Rating★★★★ 4.3/5

Key Features

  • Visual scenario builder with drag-and-drop workflow design
  • Data transformation tools for text, math, date, and JSON manipulation
  • Error handling with retry logic, error routes, and fallback values
  • Webhook support for custom app integrations
  • App connections for 1,500+ services and APIs
  • Scheduling and triggering options (webhook, schedule, app event)

Pros

  • Visual scenario builder is more powerful than Zapier — you can map data, transform values, and route between branches
  • Free tier (1,000 operations/month) is actually usable for learning and simple automations
  • Data transformation tools (text functions, math, date parsing, JSON manipulation) rival what you'd do in code
  • Error handling is built in; retry logic, error routes, and fallback values make workflows more reliable
  • Webhook support is excellent for connecting custom apps that don't have native Make modules

Cons

  • Steeper learning curve than Zapier — the visual builder is powerful but not immediately obvious
  • Less popular than Zapier means fewer pre-built templates and smaller community
  • Operations limit (1,000 free, 10,000 Pro) can be restrictive for high-volume workflows
  • Scenario design can become messy with complex workflows; organization features are limited
  • Some app modules are community-built and less reliable than official integrations
Try Make (Integromat) →

n8n

PricingFree self-hosted / $20/mo Cloud Starter
Rating★★★★½ 4.5/5

Key Features

  • 400+ nodes for connecting apps and building workflows
  • Self-hostable with no usage limits or per-operation charges
  • Code mode for custom JavaScript processing within workflows
  • Sub-workflows for modular, reusable automation components
  • Advanced error handling with error workflows and retry logic
  • Webhook triggers and API endpoints for custom integrations

Pros

  • Self-hosted version is completely free with no usage limits, operation caps, or feature gates
  • 400+ pre-built nodes cover most popular apps and services well
  • Code mode lets you write JavaScript directly in your workflows for custom processing that visual tools can't handle
  • Error handling is sophisticated — error workflows, retry logic, and complex branching for production reliability
  • Active open-source community means frequent updates and a growing node ecosystem

Cons

  • Requires hosting (Docker on a VPS or local server) which means infrastructure you need to maintain
  • Node-based visual editor is functional but less polished than Make or Zapier
  • Documentation is improving but has gaps, especially for advanced features
  • Scaling self-hosted n8n requires database setup, backup strategies, and monitoring
  • Official cloud hosting ($20/mo) gives you 5,000 workflow executions but costs more than self-hosting

Which Should You Choose?

Both Make (Integromat) and n8n are excellent tools in their category. The right choice depends on your specific needs:

  • Choose Make (Integromat) if: Make (formerly Integromat) is the automation platform for when Zapier isn't quite powerful enough. Instead of simple if-this-then-that rules, Make gives you a visual builder where you can chain together complex workflows with data transformation, conditional logic, and error handling. It's more work to set up, but it can do things Zapier can't.
  • Choose n8n if: n8n is the open-source automation tool for people who don't want to pay per operation forever. You can self-host it on your own server and run unlimited workflows without any per-operation charges. It's more technical than Make or Zapier — you need to be comfortable with Docker and Node.js — but the freedom from usage-based pricing is liberating.
We may earn a commission if you purchase through links. All opinions are our own.
